title: Violence Against Women (VAW) and Domestic Violence
topic: Violence Against Women (VAW) and Domestic Violence

content.overview:
Violence against women (VAW) is a global public health problem and a violation of human rights. It includes physical, sexual, emotional, psychological, and economic violence perpetrated primarily by intimate partners, family members, or strangers. The most common form is intimate partner violence (IPV), also called domestic violence. In India, violence against women remains widespread, underreported, and deeply embedded in social norms. Its impact on women's physical and mental health is severe and long-lasting. Every woman experiencing violence deserves support and has legal rights.

- Globally, 1 in 3 women (30%) experience physical and/or sexual violence by an intimate partner or sexual violence by a non-partner in their lifetime.
- Violence against women is the most prevalent form of human rights violation globally.
- In India, NFHS-5 (2019–21): 30% of ever-married women aged 18–49 reported experiencing physical, sexual, or emotional violence by their spouse.
- India-specific: Dowry violence, honour killings, acid attacks, child marriage, and trafficking disproportionately affect women and girls.
- Intimate partner violence (IPV) is a leading cause of injury, death, and mental illness in women globally.
- Women who have experienced violence are more likely to have depression, PTSD, alcohol use, and suicidal thoughts.
- Only 14% of women who experience violence in India seek any kind of help (NFHS-5).
- Violence is a health emergency — not a private family matter.

Q: What is the difference between domestic violence and intimate partner violence?
A: Intimate Partner Violence (IPV) refers specifically to violence by a current or former intimate partner (husband, boyfriend). Domestic violence is broader — includes violence by any household member (in-laws, siblings, parents). In India, PWDVA covers both.

Q: Can a woman file a complaint against her husband for marital rape in India?
A: India has not yet fully criminalised marital rape. However, under PWDVA, forced sexual acts by a husband are classified as domestic violence and a woman can seek protection. The Supreme Court of India has ongoing cases on this issue.

Q: What should a woman do if she is afraid to leave an abusive partner?
A: Safety planning is critical. She should contact a helpline (181/112), reach a Sakhi/One-Stop Centre, or speak to a trusted person. She can apply for a protection order without leaving the home first. Social workers and NGOs can help plan safe exit strategies.

Q: Is staying in an abusive relationship a personal choice?
A: No. Leaving is often the most dangerous time for an abused woman. Economic dependence, fear, trauma bonding, concern for children, social stigma, and lack of support all make it complex. Do not blame women for staying — help them access support.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Violence against women is a crime and a health emergency. No woman deserves to be abused. Help is available. If you or someone you know is in immediate danger, call 112 (National Emergency) or 181 (Women's Helpline). Medical care at government hospitals is available without requiring a police complaint first. Legal, shelter, and counselling support is available at Sakhi One-Stop Centres (free, government-run).
types: Hitting, slapping, punching, kicking, pushing, choking, burning
Includes dowry violence (physical assault related to dowry demands)
Rape (including marital rape — not yet fully criminalised in India)
Gaslighting (manipulating someone to doubt their own perception of reality)
Demands for additional dowry after marriage
Harassment, physical abuse, or murder related to dowry
IPC Sections 304B (dowry death) and 498A (cruelty by husband/relatives) criminalise this
Throwing corrosive substances (acid) on victim — causes severe burns, disfigurement, blindness
Increasingly criminalised in India; acid sales regulated under IPC amendments
Girls married before 18 (legal minimum age in India)
Associated with forced sexual relations, pregnancy complications, school dropout
Khatna/Khafz: Practised by some communities in India (Bohra Muslim community); constitutes violence and is illegal
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Chronic pain syndromes (headaches, back pain, pelvic pain)
Sexually transmitted infections (from forced sexual intercourse)
Depression and anxiety disorders (very high rates)
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
warning_signs: Warning signs that a partner may be abusive:
Controlling behaviour: Who you see, where you go, what you wear, your money
Threatening harm to you, your children, or pets
Blaming you for their violent behaviour
Monitoring your phone, location, or social media
Warning signs a woman may be experiencing violence:
Partner answers for her; she seems afraid to speak freely
Extreme control by partner over finances, movement, clothing
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Previous history of violence (childhood exposure or prior abuse)
Gender inequality norms (women as property, duty of obedience)
Social acceptance of violence against women
prevention: Challenging patriarchal norms and masculinity that condone violence
Women's economic empowerment (Beti Bachao Beti Padhao, SHGs)
Engaging men and boys as allies
Community interventions that change social norms
One-Stop Centres (OSCs) / Sakhi Centres: Government-run; provide shelter, counselling, legal help, medical care
Access to legal information and free legal aid
Healthcare provider training to recognise and respond to violence
Mental health support and trauma counselling
environmental_control: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
vaccination: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
treatment_awareness_only: Emergency care for injuries — all government hospitals must treat without requiring police complaint first
Emergency contraception (within 72 hours of sexual assault): Available free at government hospitals
Free legal aid: District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) — 15100 (National Legal Services Authority helpline)
Protection orders from courts under PWDVA
Swadhar Greh: Government shelter homes for women in crisis
Short-stay homes under State Women and Child Welfare Departments
Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: A woman experiencing violence should seek help if:
She is in immediate danger — call 112 (National Emergency) or 1091 (Women's Helpline) immediately
She is injured — go to the nearest hospital emergency (treatment must be given; police complaint not required first)
She needs legal protection — contact DLSA (15100) or Sakhi One-Stop Centre
She is experiencing mental health effects — seek counselling
If you suspect someone else is being abused:
Express concern gently and non-judgmentally: "I've noticed some things that worried me. Are you safe?"
Do NOT pressure them or confront the abuser (may escalate danger)
Share helpline numbers and let them decide when and how to act
Seek medical care for severe, persistent, worsening, or red-flag symptoms. Use emergency number 112 for life-threatening situations.
india_specific_context: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 (PWDVA): Covers physical, sexual, economic, and emotional violence; provides for protection orders, residence orders, and maintenance
IPC Section 498A: Cruelty by husband or relatives (cognisable, non-bailable)
IPC Sections 375/376: Rape and sexual assault
Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act (POCSO) 2012: Sexual violence against children
Sexual Harassment at Workplace Act (POSH Act) 2013
Mahila Police Volunteers: Community-level support in some states
One-Stop Centres (Sakhi Centres): ~750+ across India; provide integrated services under Ministry of Women and Child Development
Nirbhaya Fund: Government fund for women's safety initiatives
Fast-Track Special Courts (FTSCs): For rape and POCSO cases — reduce trial time
Beti Bachao Beti Padhao: Campaign addressing sex-selective practices and girl child education
NCW (National Commission for Women): ncw.nic.in — complaints and advocacy
COVID-19 and VAW: Domestic violence increased significantly during lockdowns (2020–21); government extended OSC reach
